section.title: Ruth at the Threshing Floor
section.sectionRationale: The third act is the bold night at the threshing floor. Naomi plans a resting place for Ruth, Ruth appeals to Boaz to spread his garment over her as redeemer, and Boaz responds with integrity, promising to settle the matter while honoring a nearer kinsman first. It is one tense, tender movement in which the request for redemption is made and answered in principle.
section.thesis: A vulnerable widow risks everything to appeal to her redeemer for covering and rest, and the worthy Boaz answers her faith not with exploitation but with honor, a pledge, and the promise that he will not rest until redemption is secured.
section.frameNote: Four beats: Naomi's plan for Ruth's rest; the appeal at the threshing floor, spread your garment over me; Boaz's honorable response and pledge; and Ruth's return with the sign of six measures of barley.

units.label: Naomi's plan for Ruth's rest
units.note: should I not seek a resting place for you

verses.text: One day Ruth’s mother-in-law Naomi said to her, “My daughter, should I not seek a {{menuchah|resting place}} for you, that it may be well with you?
Now is not Boaz, with whose servant girls you have been working, a relative of ours? In fact, tonight he is winnowing barley on the threshing floor.
Therefore wash yourself, put on perfume, and wear your best clothes. Go down to the threshing floor, but do not let the man know you are there until he has finished eating and drinking.
When he lies down, note the place where he lies. Then go in and uncover his feet, and lie down, and he will explain to you what you should do.”
“I will do everything you say,” Ruth answered.
So she went down to the threshing floor and did everything her mother-in-law had instructed her to do.

units.label: The appeal at the threshing floor
units.note: spread the corner of your garment over me

verses.text: After Boaz had finished eating and drinking and was in good spirits, he went to lie down at the end of the heap of grain. Then Ruth went in secretly, uncovered his feet, and lay down.
At midnight, Boaz was startled, turned over, and there lying at his feet was a woman!
“Who are you?” he asked. “I am your servant Ruth,” she replied. “Spread the corner of your {{kanaph|garment}} over me, for you are a {{goel|kinsman-redeemer}}.”
Then Boaz said, “May the LORD bless you, my daughter. You have shown more {{chesed|kindness}} now than before, because you have not run after the younger men, whether rich or poor.
And now do not be afraid, my daughter. I will do for you whatever you request, since all my fellow townspeople know that you are a woman of {{chayil|noble character}}.
Yes, it is true that I am a kinsman-redeemer, but there is a redeemer nearer than I.
Stay here tonight, and in the morning, if he wants to redeem you, good. Let him redeem you. But if he does not want to redeem you, as surely as the LORD lives, I will. Now lie here until morning.”
So she lay down at his feet until morning, but she got up before anyone else could recognize her. Then Boaz said, “Do not let it be known that a woman came to the threshing floor.”
And he told her, “Bring the shawl you are wearing and hold it out.” When she did so, he poured in six measures of barley and placed it on her. Then he went into the city.
When Ruth returned to her mother-in-law, Naomi asked her, “How did it go, my daughter?” Then Ruth told her all that Boaz had done for her.
And she said, “He gave me these six measures of barley, for he said, ‘Do not go back to your mother-in-law empty-handed.’”
“Wait, my daughter,” said Naomi, “until you find out how things go, for he will not rest unless he has resolved the matter today.”

menuchah.gloss: Rest, a resting place, security, a settled home. Naomi seeks a menuchah for Ruth, the security of marriage and a household after years of vulnerability. The idea of God-given rest runs through Scripture, from the promised land to the deep rest Christ gives to the weary, Matthew 11:28.

kanaph.gloss: Wing, edge, or corner of a garment. Ruth asks Boaz to spread the corner of his garment, his kanaph, over her, a gesture of protection and a proposal of marriage. It deliberately echoes 2:12, where Boaz blessed Ruth for taking refuge under the wings, kanaph, of God; now she asks him to be the very shelter he prayed for.

goel.gloss: Kinsman-redeemer or guardian-redeemer, the near relative legally responsible to rescue his kin, buy back family land, and raise up a name for the dead. The BSB footnote points to Leviticus 25:25-55. Ruth appeals to Boaz precisely as a goel, asking him to take up the redeemer's role for her and Naomi.

chesed.gloss: Loving devotion, steadfast love, loyal kindness beyond duty. Boaz praises Ruth's later kindness, her chesed, in seeking a redeemer for the family's sake rather than chasing younger men. The word ties her act to the book's great theme and to the covenant faithfulness that God Himself shows.

chayil.gloss: Strength, worth, valor, noble character. Boaz calls Ruth an eshet chayil, a woman of noble character, the same phrase used of the excellent woman in Proverbs 31:10 and the counterpart to his own description as a gibbor chayil in 2:1. The whole town knows her worth; a worthy woman meets a worthy man.

basic.context: Naomi wants to secure Ruth's future, so she sends her at night to the threshing floor where Boaz is sleeping after the winnowing. Following Naomi's plan, Ruth lies at his feet and, when he wakes, asks him to spread his garment over her because he is a kinsman-redeemer, in effect a proposal of marriage and a plea for covering. Boaz responds with honor and joy, promises to act, but tells her there is a nearer redeemer who must be given first refusal. He sends her home with grain and a pledge to settle it that very day.
basic.hermeneutics: This is a tense, carefully told scene that modern readers can misread. The language is charged but the narrative goes out of its way to show that both Ruth and Boaz act with integrity. Ruth's request to spread the garment is covenant language for marriage and protection, not seduction. Boaz's care to honor the nearer kinsman shows righteousness, not reluctance.
basic.originalLanguages: Naomi seeks a menuchah, a resting place, for Ruth. Ruth asks Boaz to spread his kanaph, the corner of his garment, over her, the same word for the wings of God under which she took refuge in chapter two. She appeals to him as a goel, a kinsman-redeemer. Boaz praises her chesed, loyal kindness, and calls her an eshet chayil, a woman of noble character.
basic.typology: Boaz spreading his garment over Ruth pictures Christ covering His people, taking them as His own and giving them rest. Ruth's bold approach in humility and faith mirrors the way sinners come to Christ, casting themselves on the mercy and right of a redeemer who is able and willing to save.
basic.christ: Boaz, willing to redeem and covering Ruth with his garment, foreshadows Jesus, who covers our shame with His own righteousness and takes us as His bride. Just as Boaz would not rest until the matter of redemption was settled, Christ did not rest until our redemption was accomplished at the cross.
basic.god: God's providence keeps working through wise plans and faithful people. We learn that God gives rest and security to the vulnerable, that He honors integrity, and that He is drawing the whole situation toward redemption, even though His name is spoken more in blessing than in visible action.
basic.crossReferences: Ezekiel 16 pictures God spreading His garment over His people in covenant love. Proverbs 31 celebrates the woman of noble character Ruth is called. And Christ's covering of His bride appears in Ephesians 5 and Revelation 19.
basic.oneStory: The redemption story tightens as Ruth asks to be covered by her redeemer and Boaz pledges to act. The garment spread over the widow points ahead to Christ covering His people and giving them rest, and Boaz's refusal to rest until it is settled foreshadows the Redeemer who finishes the work of salvation.

context.literary: The turning point of the plot, mirroring chapter two but at night and with the stakes raised from provision to marriage and redemption. The scene is built on suspense: a secret approach, a startled waking at midnight, a bold request, and an unresolved obstacle in the nearer kinsman. It ends, like chapter one, with grain and a word about waiting, propelling the story to the gate in chapter four.
context.historical: Threshing floors were open, communal places where owners often slept to guard the harvest. A woman approaching a man at night was fraught with risk to her reputation, which is why the narrative stresses secrecy and Boaz's protection of her honor. Spreading a garment over a woman was a recognized gesture of betrothal and protection, echoing the covering imagery of covenant.
context.canonical: The redeemer theme, and the covering of the vulnerable, ties this scene to the wider biblical picture of God as the One who spreads His covenant garment over His people, Ezekiel 16:8. The eshet chayil language links Ruth to Proverbs 31, and the pledge of redemption drives toward the legal resolution that secures the line of David and the Messiah.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show Ruth's faith-filled appeal for redemption and Boaz's honorable readiness to redeem, to heighten the tension with the nearer kinsman, and to portray both characters as models of covenant loyalty and integrity as the story moves toward its resolution.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The narrative describes a specific, culturally bound plan carried out with integrity; it is not a template for how believers should pursue marriage. What is prescriptive is the character on display: Ruth's humble, faith-filled boldness and Boaz's protective righteousness, along with the covenant picture of a redeemer covering the vulnerable.

debates.question: Was there anything improper in Ruth's night approach to Boaz?

views.label: A chaste, honorable appeal
views.case: The narrative underscores Ruth's virtue and Boaz's restraint; uncovering his feet and asking for his garment are covenant gestures of a marriage proposal and protection, and Boaz responds by guarding her reputation and blessing her character. Nothing immoral occurs.

views.label: A bold, risky act redeemed by integrity
views.case: The scene is charged and dangerous by design, and Ruth takes a real risk to her honor; yet both act rightly, and the very tension magnifies their integrity and God's overruling providence.

debates.bearing: Either way the text presents Ruth and Boaz as righteous, so the scene showcases faith and integrity rather than intrigue, and points to a redeemer who protects rather than exploits the vulnerable.

debates.question: What does the request to spread the garment mean?

views.label: A proposal of marriage and redemption
views.case: Spreading the corner of a garment over a woman was a recognized act of betrothal and protection; Ruth links it directly to Boaz being a kinsman-redeemer, so she is asking him to marry her and take up the redeemer's role.

views.label: An appeal for covenant covering
views.case: The gesture echoes 2:12, taking refuge under the wings of God, and Ezekiel 16:8, where God spreads His garment over His people; Ruth is asking to be brought under covenant protection.

debates.bearing: Both readings converge: Ruth asks to be covered, married, and redeemed, a request that pictures the covenant covering God gives His people in Christ.

study.originalLanguages: The scene turns on a cluster of loaded words. Naomi seeks a menuchah, a resting place or security, for Ruth, the settled rest of a home. At the threshing floor Ruth asks Boaz to spread his kanaph, the corner of his garment, over her; it is the same word used in 2:12 for the wings of God under which she took refuge, so she now asks Boaz to become the very shelter he had prayed God would be. She appeals to him as a goel, a kinsman-redeemer, invoking the legal role of Leviticus 25. Boaz responds by praising her chesed, her loyal kindness in seeking redemption for the family rather than chasing younger men, and by naming her an eshet chayil, a woman of noble character, the mirror of his own gibbor chayil in 2:1 and of the excellent woman of Proverbs 31.

typology.type: Boaz spreading his garment over Ruth
typology.fulfillment: Christ covering His people with His righteousness and taking them as His bride, Ezekiel 16:8 and Ephesians 5:25-27.

typology.type: Ruth's humble, bold appeal to her redeemer
typology.fulfillment: The sinner coming in faith to Christ, casting all on the mercy and right of the Redeemer who is willing and able to save.

typology.type: Boaz who will not rest until redemption is settled
typology.fulfillment: Christ who does not rest until He has accomplished the redemption of His people, John 19:30.

study.christ: Boaz is again the picture of Jesus, the willing and worthy Redeemer. When Ruth asks him to spread his garment over her, he covers the vulnerable widow, foreshadowing Christ who covers our shame and nakedness with His own righteousness and takes us as His bride, Ephesians 5:25-27. Boaz's integrity, protecting rather than exploiting the one who comes to him at her most vulnerable, shows the safety sinners find in Christ. And Boaz's resolve that he will not rest until the matter is settled that very day anticipates the Redeemer who pressed on to the cross and declared, It is finished, John 19:30.
study.god: God's name fills the scene in blessing even as His hand works quietly through human wisdom and faithfulness. We learn that the LORD is concerned to give rest and security to the vulnerable, that He honors integrity and covenant loyalty, and that He is steering events toward redemption without overriding the free, righteous choices of Ruth and Boaz. The God who spreads His own garment over His people, Ezekiel 16:8, is at work behind Boaz's garment spread over Ruth, showing a God who covers, protects, and settles His people in rest.

crossReferences.note: God spreads the corner of His garment over His people and enters into covenant with them, the image behind Ruth's request.
Boaz had blessed Ruth for taking refuge under the wings of God; now she asks him to be that covering, using the same word.
A wife of noble character, eshet chayil, who can find her; Ruth is publicly known as exactly such a woman.
Christ loved the church and gave Himself for her, to present her covered and spotless, the reality Boaz foreshadows.

study.oneStory: The story of redemption reaches its decisive appeal as Ruth asks her kinsman to spread his garment over her, and Boaz pledges to secure her redemption that very day. The garment spread over the widow points ahead to Christ, who covers His people in His righteousness, takes them as His bride, and gives them rest. Boaz's refusal to rest until the matter is settled foreshadows the Redeemer who finished the work of our salvation and now brings us safely home.

sermon.title: Ruth Proposes to Boaz
sermon.why: From Begg's series A Study in Ruth, this message expounds Ruth 3:6-18, the threshing floor scene, Ruth's appeal for covering, and Boaz as kinsman-redeemer foreshadowing Christ, showing how the blood of Christ covers His people. A faithful, expository handling of this movement's climax.

quiz.q: Why did Naomi send Ruth to the threshing floor?

options.text: To seek a resting place, the security of marriage and a home, through Boaz the redeemer.
options.note: Ruth 3:1 states Naomi's aim to find a resting place so it would be well with Ruth.

options.text: To glean more grain during the night.
options.note: The grain comes later as a gift; the purpose of the visit was to seek redemption and rest.

options.text: To confront Boaz about unfair wages.
options.note: There is no dispute over wages; the aim was marriage and redemption.

options.text: To ask Boaz for a loan.
options.note: Naomi sought a resting place through redemption, not money.

quiz.q: What did Ruth ask of Boaz at the threshing floor?

options.text: To spread the corner of his garment over her, because he was a kinsman-redeemer.
options.note: Ruth 3:9 records her appeal for covering and redemption, in effect a marriage proposal.

options.text: To give her a permanent job among his workers.
options.note: She sought redemption and covering, which is far more than employment.

options.text: To hide her from the nearer kinsman.
options.note: She asked for his covering as redeemer; Boaz himself later raises the nearer kinsman.

options.text: To take her back to Moab.
options.note: Ruth had bound herself to Israel and its God; she sought a home there, not a return.

quiz.q: How did Boaz respond to Ruth's request?

options.text: With blessing and honor, promising to act, but noting a nearer redeemer must be given first refusal.
options.note: Ruth 3:10-13 shows his integrity: willing to redeem, yet honoring the nearer kinsman.

options.text: By rejecting her outright.
options.note: He did not reject her; he blessed her and pledged to secure her redemption.

options.text: By taking advantage of her vulnerability.
options.note: The text stresses his integrity and protection of her honor, the opposite of exploitation.

options.text: By sending her away in anger.
options.note: He responded with kindness, blessing, and a firm promise to act.

quiz.q: What does Boaz spreading his garment over Ruth picture?

options.text: Christ covering His people with His righteousness and taking them as His own.
options.note: The covering echoes God spreading His garment over His people in covenant, Ezekiel 16:8.

options.text: A mere social custom with no deeper meaning.
options.note: It is a real custom, but Scripture uses it as a picture of covenant covering.

options.text: That Ruth had done something shameful.
options.note: The covering signals protection and betrothal, not shame.

options.text: That Boaz was claiming her property unfairly.
options.note: It is an act of protection and redemption, not a property grab.

quiz.q: What did Naomi say about Boaz at the end of the chapter?

options.text: That he would not rest until he had resolved the matter that very day.
options.note: Ruth 3:18 shows Boaz's resolve, foreshadowing the Redeemer who finishes His work.

options.text: That he had likely forgotten his promise already.
options.note: Naomi was confident he would act at once, not that he would forget.

options.text: That Ruth should look for another man.
options.note: Naomi told Ruth to wait, trusting Boaz to settle it.

options.text: That the nearer kinsman would surely refuse.
options.note: Naomi did not predict the outcome; she trusted Boaz to resolve it that day.

questions: Ruth took a humble, bold step of faith, trusting Boaz to act as her redeemer. Where is God calling you to a bold step of faith, casting yourself on His mercy?
Boaz protected Ruth's honor when he could have exploited her. How does his integrity challenge the way you treat others, especially when you hold power over them?
Ruth asked to be covered by her redeemer. How does it comfort you that Christ covers your shame with His own righteousness?
Boaz would not rest until redemption was settled. How does knowing that Christ finished the work of your salvation give you rest today?
Naomi's counsel was, wait until you see how things turn out. Where do you need to trust and wait while God works out a matter you cannot control?

translationNotes.note: Resting place renders menuchah, security and a settled home; Naomi seeks for Ruth the rest that marriage and redemption would bring.

Spread the corner of your garment translates a phrase using kanaph, wing or corner of a garment; the BSB footnote notes it can be rendered spread your wing, echoing 2:12. It is a gesture of betrothal and protection.
A woman of noble character renders eshet chayil, the same phrase as Proverbs 31:10 and the counterpart to Boaz as a man of chayil in 2:1.
Six measures of barley was a generous load; the BSB footnote suggests possibly six seahs, and the gift signals Boaz's pledge that Ruth would not go home empty-handed.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Ruth 3:1-18. Footnotes on verse 9 (garment or wing) and verse 15 (six measures) are reflected in the translation notes.
